ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a pomegranate juice entry; this product is a diluted juice drink with added sweeteners rather than pure juice. Closest available FoodOn anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers pomegranate juice; this product is a diluted juice drink with added sugar and water. Approximate match used as no specific FoodEx2 code exists for pomegranate juice drink.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for pomegranate juice drink as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (one glass), providing about 103 kcal and 22.5 g of sugar from this sweetened juice drink.

nutrient_source.title: Pomegranate juice drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for pomegranate juice drink (cr_id D03070030). All per-100g values are as reported. Saturated fat not separately reported by HPB for this entry; set to 0.0 as total fat is 0.0g confirmed zero. Calcium and iron reported as 0.0 mg by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pomegranate juice drink (diluted, sweetened). GI of 67 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fruit juice drinks and sweetened juice beverages (GI range 55–70 for sugar-sweetened juice drinks). Pomegranate juice (pure, unsweetened) has a reported GI of approximately 53 (Atkinson et al. 2008); the addition of sugar and dilution raises the effective GI. A conservative estimate of 67 (medium GI) is applied, consistent with other sugar-sweetened juice drinks in the Sydney GI database. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (67 × 25.5) / 100 = 17.1; rounded to 15.3 using 22.8g available carbohydrate per 250g serving (10.2 × 2.5 × 0.6 digestible fraction adjustment not applied — GL = 67 × 10.2 × 2.5 / 100 = 17.1 kcal; reported as 15.3 using net carbohydrate of 22.8g per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (9.0 g/100 ml) in a liquid beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention, despite zero sodium content.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened pomegranate juice drink; if pomegranate benefit is desired, choose 100% pure pomegranate juice (no added sugar) in portions of 100 ml or less, as supported by AHA 2018 guidelines on sugar-sweetened beverages and blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.0 g sugar per 100 ml and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugars are absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial blood glucose spikes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this pomegranate juice drink entirely; the high sugar load (9.0 g/100 ml) with no fibre buffer causes rapid glycaemic excursions, and a 250 ml serving delivers approximately 22.5 g of sugar with a medium-high glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased cardiovascular risk through dyslipidaemia, insulin resistance, and elevated triglycerides; this drink contains 9.0 g sugar/100 ml with no protective fibre or beneficial fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sugar-sweetened pomegranate juice drink; the added sugar content (9.0 g/100 ml) contributes to cardiovascular risk factors, and pure pomegranate juice (no added sugar) would be a preferable alternative if pomegranate polyphenol benefit is sought.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (9.0 g/100 ml) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; the diluted juice drink lacks sufficient pomegranate polyphenol concentration to provide meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should choose whole pomegranate arils or 100% pure pomegranate juice (no added sugar) rather than this diluted sweetened drink, as the added sugar negates any residual anti-inflammatory polyphenol effect.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pomegranate Juice Drink is a commercially processed beverage made from pomegranate juice concentrate reconstituted with water and sweetened with added sugar. It is not equivalent to 100% pure pomegranate juice; the dilution and added sweeteners significantly reduce the polyphenol concentration while increasing the sugar content to approximately 9.0 g per 100 ml. The drink has a sweet-tart flavour profile with a deep ruby-red colour characteristic of pomegranate. Each 100 ml provides 41 kcal, 10.2 g of carbohydrate (of which 9.0 g is sugar), and negligible protein, fat, and dietary fibre. Sodium content is effectively zero. The high sugar content with zero fibre makes this a high-glycaemic-impact beverage; liquid sugars are absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial blood glucose elevation. This product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and carries the same cardiovascular and metabolic risk profile as other SSBs. It is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Pure pomegranate juice (no added sugar) has documented evidence for blood pressure reduction and anti-inflammatory effects via punicalagins and ellagic acid; this diluted juice drink does not share those clinical benefits. The product is vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly consumed as a snack beverage or refreshment drink.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics drink pomegranate juice drink?
How much sugar is in pomegranate juice drink?
Is pomegranate juice drink the same as pomegranate juice?
Calories in pomegranate juice drink per 100ml

Is pomegranate juice drink suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D03070030, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. All core macros are confirmed from this approved source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is confirmed zero by HPB — this is a water-based sweetened juice drink containing no protein-bearing ingredients. fat_g is confirmed zero by HPB — the product contains no fat-bearing ingredients (water, juice concentrate, sugar only). dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero by HPB — the juice concentrate and added sugar contribute no dietary fibre. sodium_mg is confirmed zero by HPB. These are confirmed zero values per the HPB Singapore Foodo database, not estimated. Negative sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this product. GI note: GI of 67 is an analogous estimate based on sugar-sweetened juice drink entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific pomegranate juice drink product was found in approved sources. Ontology note: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches to pomegranate juice; no exact ontology code exists for diluted sweetened pomegranate juice drink. calcium_mg and iron_mg are set to 0.0 as explicitly reported by HPB (not estimated).
